meta.title: SF-SPAA-6: Access Governance, Cost Controls, and Operational Administration

questions.stem: Which system role is intended for creating and managing users and roles in a Snowflake account?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because USERADMIN is the system role dedicated to user and role administration. **A** manages objects, **C** is granted broadly and is not an admin role, and **D** is organization-scoped rather than the standard account role for user management. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A company wants separate billing and administration boundaries for two business units that still belong to one Snowflake organization. What is the best design?
questions.explanation: **C** is correct because accounts provide isolated administration, billing, and security boundaries inside one organization. **A**, **B**, and **D** remain within one account boundary. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/organizations-manage

questions.stem: Which command changes the active role for the current Snowflake session?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because `USE ROLE` changes the session context. The other forms are not the standard Snowflake command. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: An administrator wants a custom role hierarchy that follows Snowflake best practice. Which TWO statements are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Custom roles should typically roll up to SYSADMIN
A role can inherit privileges from roles granted to it
Only ACCOUNTADMIN can grant custom roles to other roles

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because Snowflake recommends granting custom object-management roles into the SYSADMIN hierarchy. **C** is correct because role inheritance flows upward from granted roles to containing roles. **B** is incorrect because PUBLIC should not own production objects. **D** is incorrect because roles with the right privileges such as SECURITYADMIN can grant roles. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A team needs a parameter change to apply to every new session unless a specific warehouse overrides it. Which parameter hierarchy explains this behavior most accurately?

options.text: Schema overrides warehouse, which overrides account
User overrides account, but warehouses cannot override parameters
Warehouse can override account for warehouse-scoped execution settings
Only session-level SET commands can override account parameters

questions.explanation: **C** is correct because Snowflake supports parameter hierarchies, and many execution settings can be set at account level then overridden at warehouse level. **A** is not the relevant hierarchy for warehouse execution settings, **B** is too narrow, and **D** is false because object-level overrides exist. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/parameters

questions.stem: What action can a resource monitor take when a credit threshold is reached?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because resource monitors can notify, suspend, or suspend immediately when thresholds are reached. **A**, **C**, and **D** are outside resource monitor behavior. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: A warehouse should stop only after currently running statements finish when a monthly quota is consumed. Which resource monitor action fits best?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because `SUSPEND` prevents new queries while allowing running statements to finish. **A** only notifies, **C** can stop immediately, and **D** is not the documented action. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: Which view is designed to analyze warehouse credit consumption over time in the shared ACCOUNT_USAGE schema?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because `WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY` exposes credit usage by warehouse over time. **A** is for data access auditing, **C** for logins, and **D** for storage rather than compute credits. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage

questions.stem: A FinOps lead wants better attribution of Snowflake cost to pipelines. Which TWO practices help most directly? (Select TWO)

options.text: Set query tags for pipeline jobs
Review QUERY_ATTRIBUTION_HISTORY or warehouse metering views
Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to each pipeline owner

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because query tags help identify workload ownership. **B** is correct because Snowflake usage views support cost attribution analysis. **C** does not improve attribution, and **D** violates least privilege without improving chargeback quality. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/cost-understanding-overall

questions.stem: A warehouse experiences short bursts of concurrency during business hours but is idle overnight. Which configuration usually improves concurrency without keeping extra clusters running all night?

options.text: Enable multi-cluster and set auto-suspend appropriately
Disable auto-suspend and size up permanently
Convert all queries to serverless tasks

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because multi-cluster warehouses handle bursts, and auto-suspend limits idle spend. **B** wastes credits, **C** addresses sharing use cases, and **D** does not replace interactive warehouse concurrency. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster

questions.stem: Which privilege allows a role to grant or revoke privileges on an object it does not own when acting centrally?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because `MANAGE GRANTS` enables centralized privilege administration. **A** and **C** are narrower object operations, and **D** is unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-configure

questions.stem: A role needs to automatically receive SELECT on future tables created in a schema. Which approach should be used?

options.text: Create a future grant on tables in the schema
Grant USAGE on the warehouse only

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because future grants can automatically apply privileges such as SELECT to new tables in a schema. **A** is too broad, **C** is insufficient for table access, and **D** is unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-configure

questions.stem: In a managed access schema, who controls object privilege grants by default?

options.text: The schema owner or a role with MANAGE GRANTS
Only the object creator for the first 24 hours

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because managed access centralizes grant management with the schema owner or a role that has `MANAGE GRANTS`. **A** is true for regular schemas, not managed access. **C** and **D** are incorrect restrictions. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-configure

questions.stem: A reporting role must query tables in `finance.mart` with least privilege. Which TWO grants are required at minimum before table-level SELECT becomes usable? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because a role needs database usage. **C** is correct because the role also needs schema usage before object privileges can be exercised. **A** uses non-Snowflake terminology, and **D** is far beyond least privilege. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What is the main risk of transferring OWNERSHIP on a table to another role without copying current grants?

options.text: Existing privileges can be revoked during ownership transfer

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because ownership transfer can revoke existing grants unless handled carefully with the supported grant-copy behavior. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What does a Snowflake network policy primarily control?

options.text: Which client IP addresses can connect

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because network policies restrict login access by allowed or blocked IP ranges. **A**, **C**, and **D** are not network policy functions. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: A security team wants stricter restrictions for one privileged user than the rest of the account. Which policy assignment takes precedence for that user?

options.text: The user-level network policy applies to that user
Network policies cannot be assigned to users

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because a user-level network policy can be applied to an individual user and governs that user specifically. **A**, **C**, and **D** do not match Snowflake policy behavior. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: Why do many enterprises use PrivateLink or Private Endpoint with Snowflake?

options.text: To convert warehouses into multi-cluster mode
To avoid public internet exposure for connectivity
To replace RBAC with network-based authorization
To enable Time Travel for transient tables

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because private connectivity keeps traffic off the public internet. **A**, **C**, and **D** describe unrelated features. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about network policies are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: They can use allow lists and block lists of IP addresses
They can grant table privileges when attached to a role

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because network policies evaluate allowed and blocked IP lists. **C** is correct because they affect whether authentication is allowed from a client address. **B** confuses network controls with RBAC, and **D** is incorrect because MFA is configured separately. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: A company assigns an account-level network policy and later also assigns a user-level policy to a contractor. What is the main administrative benefit of the user-level assignment?

options.text: It lets the contractor bypass all IP restrictions
It allows a tighter exception without changing the whole account policy

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because a user-level policy supports a targeted exception or tighter restriction for one identity. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated or unsafe. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: Which table type does NOT have Fail-safe but can still support Time Travel?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because transient tables omit Fail-safe while still supporting limited Time Travel. **A** includes Fail-safe, and **C** and **D** are different storage models rather than the standard answer to this retention distinction. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A team wants session-scoped scratch data that disappears automatically and is invisible to other sessions. Which table type is best?
questions.explanation: **C** is correct because temporary tables are session-specific and automatically dropped at session end. **A** and **B** persist beyond the session, and **D** is not the correct standard table category here. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: Which warehouse behavior helps reduce credit use for sporadic ad hoc workloads?

options.text: Force all queries onto a larger warehouse

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because auto-suspend and auto-resume prevent paying for idle warehouse time. **A** increases idle spend, **C** may raise cost, and **D** addresses storage, not compute idleness. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: Which TWO characteristics are true of Snowflake multi-cluster warehouses? (Select TWO)

options.text: They add clusters to handle concurrency
They are mainly for a single long-running query
They can reduce queueing during bursty workloads
They eliminate the need for auto-suspend

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because extra clusters can be added for concurrent demand. **C** is correct because this reduces queueing during spikes. **B** confuses concurrency with individual-query size, and **D** is incorrect because auto-suspend is still useful. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster

questions.stem: Why might an administrator lower `DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS` on transient staging objects?

options.text: To reduce storage cost associated with retained history

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because shorter retention can reduce storage retained for historical versions. **A** is the opposite, and **C** and **D** are unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: Which statement best describes warehouse resizing in Snowflake?

options.text: Resizing changes compute capacity independently of storage
Resizing permanently changes database failover settings
Resizing is only available for serverless features

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because Snowflake separates storage from compute, so warehouse size affects compute resources, not table storage layout directly. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view is most appropriate for reviewing login attempts and client connection details?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because `LOGIN_HISTORY` records authentication events and client details. **A** covers queries, **C** covers object access, and **D** covers tags. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage

questions.stem: Which view helps determine which tables were read by a specific statement for audit purposes?
questions.explanation: **C** is correct because `ACCESS_HISTORY` tracks data access details by query. **A** is for login events, **B** for statement metadata without the same object-level audit depth, and **D** is not the right audit source. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage

questions.stem: A compliance officer wants to know who changed a masking policy definition yesterday. Which general Snowflake evidence source is most appropriate first?

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE views and relevant object history

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because administrative and usage history views are the right starting point for auditing configuration changes. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not provide the same governance record. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage

questions.stem: Which TWO practices strengthen Snowflake audit readiness? (Select TWO)

options.text: Review access and login history regularly
Grant ACCOUNTADMIN broadly so more people can investigate incidents
Use consistent query tagging for important workloads
Rely only on warehouse size changes as evidence

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because regular review of access and login data supports incident response. **C** is correct because query tags improve traceability. **B** weakens security, and **D** is not an audit strategy. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage

questions.stem: Which organization-level capability is unique to ORGADMIN compared with standard account roles?

options.text: Creating new databases in an account
Creating and viewing accounts in the organization

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because ORGADMIN operates at the organization level for account management. **A**, **C**, and **D** are standard account activities. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/organizations-manage

questions.stem: What is the main difference between `SUSPEND` and `SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE` actions on a resource monitor?

options.text: `SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE` can stop running statements instead of waiting for completion

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because `SUSPEND_IMMEDIATE` can halt running work, whereas `SUSPEND` waits for active statements to complete. **A**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: A role has USAGE on a database and SELECT on a table but still gets an error when querying it. What privilege is most likely missing?

options.text: USAGE on the schema containing the table

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because table access also requires schema usage. **B** is not enough for reading the object, **C** is unnecessary, and **D** is unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Which scenario is the best fit for an account-level network policy?

options.text: Restrict all users to known corporate egress IP ranges
Grant row-level security on a finance table
Charge back warehouse credits to teams
Create future grants on new schemas

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because account-level network policies set a broad authentication boundary for the account. **B**, **C**, and **D** are different governance features. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: Which storage object choice is usually most appropriate for persistent production data that needs the full maximum data protection features?
questions.explanation: **C** is correct because permanent tables support the full standard protection model including Fail-safe. **A** and **B** reduce persistence or protection, and **D** is not a production table type. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: An auditor asks for evidence of which role executed a suspicious query and which warehouse it used. Which source should you inspect first?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because `QUERY_HISTORY` contains statement-level execution metadata such as user, role context, and warehouse. The others are storage or replication usage views. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage

questions.stem: A new administrator assumes ACCOUNTADMIN should own all objects to simplify operations. Why is this a poor practice?

options.text: It violates least privilege and increases blast radius for routine work
Objects owned by ACCOUNTADMIN cannot be queried
Snowflake disallows object ownership by ACCOUNTADMIN

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because using ACCOUNTADMIN broadly expands risk and goes against Snowflake least-privilege guidance. **A**, **C**, and **D** are false. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Which object is the billing target of a warehouse-level resource monitor?

options.text: The users assigned to the warehouse
The credit usage consumed by that warehouse
The data retention period on its tables
The number of rows loaded into schemas

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because resource monitors track and act on credit consumption. **A**, **C**, and **D** are not their billing target. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about future grants are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: They can reduce repetitive privilege administration on new objects
They apply retroactively to all existing objects automatically
They are useful for schemas that receive new tables frequently
They remove the need for USAGE privileges on parent containers

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because future grants automate recurring grants. **C** is correct because they help when new objects are created regularly. **B** is incorrect because they are not retroactive for all existing objects, and **D** is incorrect because parent container usage privileges are still required. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-configure

questions.stem: A warehouse serving dashboard queries is frequently idle for 12 minutes between morning refresh bursts. Which setting is most likely to reduce waste without harming usability?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because shortening auto-suspend cuts idle compute cost while auto-resume restores usability. **B** would hurt usability, and **C** and **D** do not address idle warehouse cost. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview
